---
title: "Git Activity"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
---

```{r}
#| include: false
framework::scaffold()
```

# Git and GitHub Activity

## Goal

Complete one full local-to-remote Git workflow by hand.

## What You Will Do

1. Create a new project folder.
2. Create a file.
3. Initialize a Git repository.
4. Add a remote GitHub repository.
5. Stage and commit a file.
6. Push to GitHub.
7. Create and test a `.gitignore`.
8. Clone the repository into a second directory.
9. Make a change there.
10. Push the change and pull it back into the original directory.

## Before You Start

You will need:

1. Git installed on your machine.
2. A GitHub account.
3. A new empty repository on GitHub.

For practice in class, we will use a public repository.

In real research workflows, you will often use private repositories instead, especially when the project itself is sensitive.

Use your own repository URL anywhere you see `[YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME]` below.

Use your own folder name anywhere you see `[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]` below.

Do not copy and paste these commands exactly as written. Typing things out yourself will help you understand what you are doing.

I use terminal commands here, but you can use your editor or the `GitHub Desktop` application to create directories and files if you prefer.

## Authentication

If your repository is private, GitHub will require authentication when you push, pull, or clone.

If it is public, you will also need to authenticate to be able to push to it. (Otherwise anyone from the general public woudl be allowed to overwrite your work!)

When Git prompts you for credentials over HTTPS:

1. Your username is your GitHub username.
2. Your password is a personal access token, not your GitHub account password.

For this class, the most straightforward option is usually a classic personal access token.

To create one on GitHub:

1. Log in to GitHub in your browser.
2. Click your profile picture in the top right.
3. Go to `Settings`.
4. In the left sidebar, click `Developer settings`.
5. Click `Personal access tokens`.
6. Click `Tokens (classic)`.
7. Click `Generate new token`, then `Generate new token (classic)`.

Key details:

1. Give the token a clear name, such as `git-class-activity`.
2. Set an expiration date. A short expiration is safer, because if you leak your token, it will expire and be useless. Long expirations are convenient, but more risky.
3. Under scopes, check `repo` if you need to work with private repositories.
4. If you are only working with public repositories, checking `public_repo` is enough.
5. You do not need to check others boxes for this activity; these are for large projects with bigger needs than standard small-team or solo scientific work.

After GitHub shows you the token:

1. Copy it immediately. GitHub will not show it to you again.
2. Store it somewhere safe, such as a password manager like 1password or BitWarden.
3. When Git asks for your password, paste the token and hit einter. (In a terminal, it will often show a little key icon or something similar. It will not show anything when you type; that is normal. Just paste and hit enter.)

Other options also exist, including signing in through your browser or using SSH keys, but a classic personal access token is usually the easiest way to understand what Git is asking for.

If you work with Git and GitHub a lot, is is worth researching `.gitconfig` on your machine, which can help you avoid this setup.

Treat your personal access tokens like passwords, because they are. **Never put them in remote repositories, public or private.**

## Step 1: Create a Project Folder

Terminal:

```bash
mkdir [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]
cd [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]
```

You can also just use your editor.

## Step 2: Create a File

In your editor, create a new file, such as `README.md`.

Type something and save it.

## Step 3: Initialize Git

```bash
git init
git status
```

Check that Git now sees `README.md` as an untracked file.

## Branches: What They Mean

A branch is a named line of development in a Git repository.

You can think of a branch as a pointer to a sequence of commits. It lets you keep track of one version of the project while other branches can hold other versions.

For this activity, you will mostly work on the default branch, which is usually `main`.

You may also see the name `master` in older tutorials or older repositories.

`main` and `master` mean the same basic thing here: the primary branch of the repository.

The difference is mostly naming:

1. `master` was the older default branch name in many Git and GitHub workflows.
2. `main` is the newer standard default branch name on GitHub.

That is why some tutorials say:

```bash
git push -u origin master
```

but in this activity we will use:

```bash
git push -u origin main
```

If your repository uses `master` instead of `main`, use that branch name consistently in your commands.

## Step 4: Create a Repository on GitHub

In your browser:

1. Log in to GitHub.
2. Go to `Repositories` and click the green `New` button.
4. Enter `[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]` as the repository name.
5. For this class exercise, make it `Public`.
6. Do not initialize the repository with a README, `.gitignore`, or license.
7. Click `Create repository`.

## Step 5: Connect Your Local Repository to GitHub

After GitHub creates the empty repository, run:

```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/[YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME]/[YOUR-PROJECT-
NAME].git
```

You can verify the remote with:

```bash
git remote -v
```

## Step 6: Stage and Commit

```bash
git add README.md
git commit -m "Initial commit"
```

## Step 7: Push to GitHub

```bash
git push -u origin main
```

Here, `-u` means `--set-upstream`.

That tells Git to remember that your local `main` branch is connected to `origin/main`.

After that, you can usually just run:

```bash
git push
git pull
```

## Step 8: Create `.gitignore`

In your editor, create a new file named `.gitignore`.

Add this line to `.gitignore`:

```text
data/
```

## Step 9: Test `.gitignore`

In the terminal:

```bash
mkdir data
git status
```

In your editor or file browser, create a file named something like `private-data.csv` inside the `data` folder.

Check that `data/private-data.csv` does not appear in `git status`.

Now add and commit `.gitignore` itself:

```bash
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Add gitignore"
git push
```

## Step 10: Clone Into a Second Directory

Move back out of the folder and clone the repository somewhere else:

```bash
cd ..
git clone https://github.com/[YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME]/[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME].git [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]-copy
cd [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]-copy
```

## Step 11: Make a Change in the Copy

In your editor, create a new file named `notes.md`.

Type one line of text and save it.

```bash
git add notes.md
git commit -m "Add notes file"
git push
```

## Step 12: Pull the Change Into the Original Directory

```bash
cd ../[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]
git pull
```

Confirm that the new file is now present.

## AI Tip

AI tools are really good at Git.

Good uses include:

1. asking what a command does
2. asking how to fix a common Git error
3. asking what sequence of commands you need next


## Status

You should end with:

1. a local repository
2. at least two commits
3. a remote GitHub repository
4. one ignored file or directory
5. a successful push and pull workflow across two local copies
